character.name: Maya Ortiz-Chen

appearance.brief: Medium height, curly dark brown hair she's constantly pulling into a messy bun and then taking back down, warm brown skin, wears mismatched socks on purpose 'as a personality thing.' Still has a slightly-too-big backpack from last year because her mom said it 'still has good use in it.'
distinctiveFeatures: A friendship bracelet from her old best friend Priya that she still wears even though they haven't texted in three weeks
Bites the inside of her cheek when nervous instead of her nails (a habit she's proud of, calls it 'the mature version')
Doodles tiny cartoon eyes on the corner of every worksheet

mannerisms: Rehearses conversations in her head before she has them, mouthing words slightly
Laughs a beat too early at jokes when she's trying to seem chill
Straightens her backpack straps repeatedly when entering a new room
speechPatterns: Uses 'okay so' to buy thinking time before answering a question
Slides into sarcasm when she feels exposed ('Oh great, love that for me')
Talks faster and more when nervous, then catches herself and goes quiet
personality.emotionalTendencies: Maya performs confidence before she feels it — she's the kid who can walk into a room and seem totally fine, then go cry in the bathroom stall five minutes later. She reads social rooms like a sport she's good at, which makes her feel powerful and exhausted at the same time. She swings between genuinely warm and defensively cool depending on how safe she feels.

background.family: Only child of a Mexican-American mom (ER nurse, works irregular shifts) and Chinese-American dad (recently laid off, now 'figuring things out' from home, which everyone pretends isn't stressful). They moved three towns over so Mom could take a promotion — Maya didn't get a vote, and she knows it.
interests: making stop-motion videos with her old figurines
playing the trumpet badly but enthusiastically in band
strengths: fiercely defends anyone she decides is 'hers'
struggles: conflates being liked with being safe
abandons her real opinions to match whoever she's with
hasn't told anyone how much she misses her old school because she thinks that makes her sound ungrateful
secrets: She already texted her old best friend Priya 'this school sucks' on day one before even finding a seat at lunch — a lie she's now stuck maintaining
She's scared that if people here knew the real her — the one who cries at commercials and researches octopuses for fun — they'd like the performed version better

conscious: Find a place to sit at lunch that doesn't feel like a mistake
Not be 'the new girl' by Thanksgiving
Make at least one friend who isn't just proximity-based
unconscious: Prove to herself that she can be liked for who she actually is, not just who she pretends to be
Feel some sense of control after a move she didn't choose

voiceGuidelines.vocabularyLevel: Contemporary 12-year-old — casual, some slang ('lowkey,' 'that's so real,' 'not me doing X'), but not try-hard slang-for-slang's-sake. Occasional oddly formal word when she's nervous and overcorrecting.
voiceGuidelines.sentenceComplexity: Short, punchy internal thoughts; run-on sentences when rambling from nerves; trails off with '...' when unsure.
voiceGuidelines.emotionalExpressiveness: Says less than she feels out loud, but her internal monologue (player choices/thoughts) is much more raw and honest than her outward performance — that gap IS the character.
examples: "Okay so. New school. Totally normal. I've done this before. (I have never done this before.)"
"I could sit with them. Or I could sit with the kid nobody's talking to and just... see what happens. Low stakes. Right?"
"I'm fine. I'm SO fine. Why does everyone keep asking if I'm fine."
"Jordan's not weird. Jordan's just... doing his own thing. Honestly? Kind of respect it."

character.storyRole: Player-character protagonist. Her choices in 'First Impressions' — how she introduces herself, who she sits with, how she treats Jordan — are the primary mechanism through which the player experiences the episode's themes.
arcPotential: Episode 1 (First Impressions): Decides between easy social safety and genuine connection with Jordan; sets the tone for whether she's building a real friendship or a convenient one.

Episode 2-3: Semester project partnership with Jordan forces her to either keep performing 'chill new girl' or let him see her actual interests (the ocean facts, the stop-motion videos) — risk of real intimacy vs. safety of surface-level ease.

Mid-season: Old friend group back home starts drifting, forcing Maya to confront whether she's been more honest with Jordan than with people who supposedly know her — a moment of realizing authenticity isn't about location, it's a choice she keeps making.
Finale potential: A moment where the 'easy' popular-adjacent seat and the 'real' friendship seat are mutually exclusive — Maya has to choose publicly, not just in her head.

designNotes: Maya's central engine is the gap between performed confidence and internal anxiety — a very true-to-12-year-old contradiction. She's not a blank self-insert; she has specific texture (the lie to Priya, the trumpet, the octopus facts) that gives writers something to play with and gives players someone to *become* rather than just steer. Her defense mechanism — social chameleoning — is directly the obstacle the episode needs her to overcome to actually connect with Jordan. Her arc isn't 'learn to be confident,' it's 'learn that being liked for a performance isn't the same as belonging,' which ties directly to all three episode themes without being a lecture.
representationNotes: Maya is mixed-heritage (Mexican-American/Chinese-American) in a way that's present in texture (family details, food, names) but not the plot's central conflict — her story is about belonging in a new social world, not a story ABOUT her ethnicity, which avoids reducing her to a single-issue character. Her dad's recent job loss is included as ambient family stress (common for this age group to sense but not fully understand) without becoming an after-school-special subplot — it's texture that explains why the move happened and why she feels destabilized, not a 'lesson' the episode teaches through her family.
